---

sections.title: L7 — Ecosystem
sections.content:
### L7: Hydronic Heating Equipment Ecosystem

The complete ecosystem of interdependent OEMs, component suppliers, installers, energy utilities, regulators, and building designers coordinated around the manufacture and deployment of hydronic (water-based) heating equipment — excluding warm air furnaces, heat pumps, and refrigeration equipment (which fall under NAICS 333415). Encompasses gas/oil boilers, hydronic baseboard heaters, radiant floor systems, solar thermal collectors, and pool heaters. The U.S. industry comprises ~670 active establishments with ~14,800 employees and ~$4.4B in revenue (Census 2017). The global residential boiler market alone exceeds $19B (2025) with a CAGR of ~6% driven by condensing technology mandates and energy efficiency regulations. [SRC: web_search, Census Bureau]

---

sections.title: L6 — System-of-Systems Decomposition
sections.content:
Seven L6 domains follow the energy path from combustion/conversion source through hydraulic distribution to occupant comfort, plus enabling domains.

| L6 ID | Name | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L6.1** | Heat Generation | Core | Combustion, electric resistance, or solar thermal conversion subsystems that produce thermal energy in a hydronic medium. Technology choice here defines appliance type. |
| **L6.2** | Hydraulic Distribution | Core | Piping, pumping, sensing, and safety infrastructure moving heated water from source to emitters and back. **Marquardt sits here.** |
| **L6.3** | Heat Emission | Core | Terminal devices (baseboard, radiators, radiant floor, fan coils) transferring heat from water to occupied space. |
| **L6.4** | Control & Regulation | Core | Intelligence layer: appliance controllers, thermostats, zone valves, weather compensation, BUS communication. |
| **L6.5** | Domestic Hot Water (DHW) | Core | Potable water heating via storage cylinders, plate heat exchangers, thermostatic mixing, legionella protection. |
| **L6.6** | Installation, Commissioning & Aftermarket | Horizontal | Human-process layer: installer tools, system flushing, commissioning, warranty service, spare parts. |
| **L6.7** | Energy Monitoring & Compliance | Horizontal | Thermal energy metering, efficiency verification, ErP/EcoDesign labelling, smart-grid integration. |

---

sections.title: L6.1 — Heat Generation
sections.content:
The primary energy-conversion domain. For NAICS 333414, this means combustion (gas/oil), electric resistance, or solar thermal — NOT refrigerant-based (heat pumps are 333415).

### L5 Systems under L6.1

#### L5.1.1: Gas Burner Assembly

Premix or atmospheric burner converting natural gas or propane into thermal energy. Modulating premix burners with 1:5 to 1:10 turndown dominate condensing boilers. [SRC: web_search]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.1.1.1: Premix Burner | Modulating surface burner with metal-fibre or ceramic-fibre deck; air/gas premixing upstream |
| L4.1.1.2: Gas Valve Train | Modulating gas valve (Honeywell, SIT, Dungs), gas pressure regulator, shut-off valve |
| L4.1.1.3: Ignition & Flame Detection | Hot-surface igniter or spark electrode; ionisation flame sensor for flame proving |
| L4.1.1.4: Combustion Air Fan | Variable-speed EC blower providing forced draft and air/fuel ratio control |

#### L5.1.2: Primary Heat Exchanger

Transfers combustion energy to hydronic water. Condensing boilers use stainless steel (316L) or aluminium-silicon alloy to withstand acidic condensate (pH 3–5). The single most expensive component in a condensing boiler. [SRC: web_search]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.1.2.1: Condensing Heat Exchanger Core | Stainless steel or Al-Si spiral/plate exchanger extracting latent heat from flue gases; achieves 95–98% efficiency |
| L4.1.2.2: Primary (Sensible) Heat Exchanger | Cast iron, copper, or steel fire-tube/water-tube exchanger for sensible heat transfer; used in non-condensing boilers |
| L4.1.2.3: Condensate Trap & Drain | Collects acidic condensate from flue-gas cooling; neutraliser required before drain discharge |

#### L5.1.3: Oil Burner Assembly

Pressure-atomising or rotary-cup oil burner for oil-fired boilers. Declining market share in North America/EU but significant installed base.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.1.3.1: Oil Nozzle & Atomiser | Pressure-atomising nozzle (Danfoss, Delavan) converting fuel oil into fine spray |
| L4.1.3.2: Oil Pump & Pre-Heater | Gear pump with pressure regulator; pre-heater for viscous fuels |
| L4.1.3.3: Ignition Transformer & Electrode | High-voltage transformer and spark electrodes for oil ignition |

#### L5.1.4: Electric Heating Element

Immersion or flanged resistance heating element for electric hydronic boilers. Simpler construction than combustion; no flue required.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.1.4.1: Resistance Heating Element | Incoloy or stainless steel sheathed element; typical 5–30 kW residential |
| L4.1.4.2: Power Contactor & Staging | Solid-state or mechanical contactors for multi-stage element switching |
| L4.1.4.3: Over-Temperature Safety Cut-Out | Manual-reset thermal cut-out preventing dry-fire or runaway heating |

#### L5.1.5: Solar Thermal Collector

Flat-plate or evacuated-tube solar collector for solar water heating systems. Pre-heats hydronic circuit or DHW storage.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.1.5.1: Absorber Plate & Riser Tubes | Selective-coated copper or aluminium absorber with copper riser tubes |
| L4.1.5.2: Glazing & Insulation | Low-iron tempered glass cover; mineral wool or foam back insulation |
| L4.1.5.3: Solar Pump Station | Circulator, flow meter, non-return valve, pressure relief, and fill/drain valves in a compact unit |

#### L5.1.6: Flue Gas System

Exhaust path from combustion chamber to atmosphere. Concentric (room-sealed) flues dominate modern installations.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.1.6.1: Concentric Flue Terminal | Room-sealed balanced-flue terminal allowing combustion air intake and exhaust via single wall penetration |
| L4.1.6.2: Flue Duct & Extensions | Stainless steel or polypropylene (condensing-rated) duct sections |
| L4.1.6.3: Condensate Neutraliser | Calcium carbonate (limestone) cartridge neutralising acidic condensate to pH 5–7 before drain |

---

sections.title: L6.2 — Hydraulic Distribution
sections.content:
The water-side infrastructure connecting heat source to emitters and back. **This is where Marquardt's ultrasonic flow sensor sits.** Every litre of heated water passes through L6.2 components; hydraulic performance directly determines delivered efficiency and occupant comfort.

### L5 Systems under L6.2

#### L5.2.1: Circulator Pump

Prime mover of the hydronic loop. Modern systems use high-efficiency EC circulators (Wilo, Grundfos ~70% combined EU share) with integrated differential-pressure control. [SRC: web_search]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.2.1.1: EC Motor & Impeller | Permanent-magnet electronically commutated motor with wet-rotor design; 4–80 W typical |
| L4.2.1.2: Speed Controller / PWM Interface | Integrated inverter with 0–10 V, PWM, or BUS interface for external modulation |
| L4.2.1.3: Pump Housing & Connections | Cast iron or composite volute; DN20–DN32 threaded or flanged connections |

#### L5.2.2: Hydraulic Separator

Decouples primary (heat-source) circuit from secondary (distribution) circuit. Essential in multi-zone systems.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.2.2.1: Separator Vessel | Steel or stainless-steel chamber with internal baffles for flow decoupling and air/dirt separation |
| L4.2.2.2: Air Vent & Drain | Automatic air vent (top); drain valve (bottom) for sludge removal |
| L4.2.2.3: Insulation Jacket | Thermal insulation shell preventing standing heat losses |

#### L5.2.3: Distribution Manifold

Multi-port header splitting primary supply into individual zone circuits. Particularly important for radiant floor heating.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.2.3.1: Manifold Bar & Zone Outlets | Brass or stainless manifold with balancing valves and flow meters per circuit |
| L4.2.3.2: Zone Valve Actuators | Thermal or motor-driven actuators on each loop; respond to room thermostat signals |
| L4.2.3.3: Manifold Cabinet & Insulation | Wall-mount enclosure with condensation protection and thermal insulation |

#### L5.2.4: Piping Network

Physical conduit for heated water. Material depends on application: copper for primary, PEX/PE-RT for radiant loops, multilayer composite for risers.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.2.4.1: Primary Circuit Piping | Copper or stainless steel from heat source to separator/manifold; typically DN22–DN28 |
| L4.2.4.2: Secondary / Zone Piping | PEX, PE-RT, or multilayer composite for zone circuits; DN12–DN20 |
| L4.2.4.3: Pipe Fittings & Joints | Press fittings, compression fittings, solder joints; increasingly push-fit |
| L4.2.4.4: Pipe Insulation | Closed-cell elastomeric or polyethylene foam per EN 12828 / ASHRAE 90.1 |

#### L5.2.5: Flow Sensing & Measurement

Instruments measuring volumetric flow rate, flow direction, medium temperature, and medium condition within the hydronic loop. The flow signal feeds appliance controllers for safety interlocks, circulator modulation, thermal energy calculation (Q = m-dot x cp x delta-T), and anomaly detection. [SRC: web_search]

**Marquardt's ultrasonic flow sensor sits at L4 within this L5 node.**

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| **L4.2.5.1: Ultrasonic Flow Sensor** | **Transit-time ultrasonic measurement using opposed piezoelectric transducers. No moving parts, zero pressure drop, glycol-tolerant, air/empty-pipe detection. Marquardt DN20 is the reference product. Growing adoption in modern condensing boilers.** |
| L4.2.5.2: Impeller / Turbine Flow Sensor | Mechanical paddle-wheel or turbine rotor; incumbent in many boiler platforms. Moving parts subject to wear and scaling. Declining share. |
| L4.2.5.3: Vortex Flow Sensor | Bluff-body vortex shedding; higher accuracy at mid-range flows but introduces pressure drop. Niche. |
| L4.2.5.4: Differential Pressure Flow Estimation | Inferred from pump speed and delta-P; zero hardware cost but low accuracy (±15–20%). Budget fallback. |

#### L5.2.6: Expansion & Safety Devices

Pressure management and safety relief protecting the closed hydronic loop.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.2.6.1: Expansion Vessel | Diaphragm vessel absorbing volumetric expansion; pre-charged nitrogen; 8–18 L residential |
| L4.2.6.2: Safety Valve (Pressure Relief) | Spring-loaded valve at 3 bar (typical); discharges to tundish on overpressure |
| L4.2.6.3: Pressure Gauge & Transducer | Analogue gauge for visual check; electronic transducer for controller alarms |
| L4.2.6.4: Fill Valve & Backflow Preventer | Maintains system pressure; prevents potable water contamination per EN 1717 |

#### L5.2.7: Water Treatment & Filtration

Protects hydronic loop from corrosion, scaling, and sludge. Critical in retrofit installations.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.2.7.1: Magnetic Dirt Separator | In-line magnetic filter capturing magnetite and ferrous debris |
| L4.2.7.2: System Filter / Strainer | Mesh or cyclone filter for non-ferrous particles; installed on return line |
| L4.2.7.3: Chemical Inhibitor Dosing | Corrosion/scale inhibitor dosed at commissioning and annual service |

---

sections.title: L6.3 — Heat Emission
sections.content:
Terminal devices transferring thermal energy from hydronic medium to occupied space. Emitter choice constrains supply temperature: radiant floor 30–45 °C, baseboard/radiator 50–70 °C, fan coil 40–60 °C.

### L5 Systems under L6.3

#### L5.3.1: Hydronic Baseboard Heater

Finned-tube convector in painted steel housing. Cooler air enters bottom, warms over fins, rises from top — primarily convective heat transfer. Hydronic baseboard holds ~46% of the baseboard heater market. Key manufacturers: Slant/Fin, Runtal, Marley (Regal Beloit). [SRC: web_search]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.3.1.1: Copper Tube & Aluminium Fin Element | Copper carrier tube with press-fit aluminium fins; heat output rated per linear foot at standard delta-T |
| L4.3.1.2: Steel Enclosure & End Caps | Painted steel housing with inlet/outlet damper slots for convection airflow |
| L4.3.1.3: Isolation & Balancing Valves | Supply/return shut-off valves and balancing valve for zone flow regulation |

#### L5.3.2: Panel Radiator

Steel panel radiators with convector fins. Dominant emitter in European retrofit markets. Types 11 through 33. Manufacturers: Stelrad, Purmo, Kermi, Myson.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.3.2.1: Steel Panel & Convector Assembly | Pressed-steel panels with spot-welded convector fins |
| L4.3.2.2: Thermostatic Radiator Valve (TRV) | Self-regulating wax/liquid thermostatic head; per-room temperature control |
| L4.3.2.3: Lockshield Valve & Balancing | Return-side valve for hydraulic balancing of multi-radiator circuits |

#### L5.3.3: Radiant Floor Heating

PEX/PE-RT pipe loops embedded in screed or dry-plate. Preferred for new-build with condensing boilers due to low-temperature compatibility. Market growing at ~6% CAGR to $8.9B by 2036. [SRC: web_search]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.3.3.1: Pipe Loop (PEX / PE-RT) | Oxygen-barrier pipe in serpentine or spiral; typically 16 mm OD at 100–200 mm spacing |
| L4.3.3.2: Screed / Dry-Plate Assembly | Wet screed (65–75 mm) or aluminium dry-plate system for low-profile retrofit |
| L4.3.3.3: Room Thermostat & Wiring Centre | Electronic or smart thermostat per zone; wired or wireless to manifold actuators |

#### L5.3.4: Fan Coil Unit (Hydronic)

Forced-convection emitter using water-to-air heat exchanger with EC fan. Used where rapid heat-up needed or space constrained.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.3.4.1: Water-to-Air Heat Exchanger Coil | Copper tube / aluminium fin coil |
| L4.3.4.2: EC Fan & Motor | Tangential or centrifugal fan with speed control for noise management |
| L4.3.4.3: Filter & Housing | Air filter, condensate tray (if cooling-capable), painted steel enclosure |

---

sections.title: L6.4 — Control & Regulation
sections.content:
Intelligence and communication layer orchestrating all L6 domains. Weather-compensated control with outdoor reset curves is standard in modern condensing boilers.

### L5 Systems under L6.4

#### L5.4.1: Appliance Controller / PCB

Main control board running combustion management, safety interlocks, pump sequencing, and diagnostics.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.4.1.1: Microcontroller & Firmware | ARM-based MCU running real-time control loops; OTA-updatable in connected models |
| L4.4.1.2: Power Supply & Relay Board | Transformer/SMPS, relay drivers for gas valve, fan, circulator, zone valves |
| L4.4.1.3: Sensor Input Interface | ADC channels for temperature sensors (NTC/Pt1000), pressure transducers, flow sensor (UART/frequency) |

#### L5.4.2: Room Thermostat / User Interface

Occupant-facing control: simple programmable through smart/connected (Nest, Ecobee, Honeywell Home).

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.4.2.1: Temperature Sensor & Display | NTC or thermistor sensor; LCD/e-ink display with setpoint indication |
| L4.4.2.2: Communication Module | Wired (2-wire, OpenTherm, eBUS) or wireless (868 MHz, Zigbee, Wi-Fi) |
| L4.4.2.3: Scheduling & Learning Logic | Time-programme scheduling; self-learning occupancy in smart variants |

#### L5.4.3: Weather Compensation Module

Outdoor sensor + reset-curve logic adjusting supply water temperature to heating load. Prerequisite for high-efficiency condensing operation.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.4.3.1: Outdoor Temperature Sensor | NTC sensor in weatherproof housing; north-facing wall mount |
| L4.4.3.2: Heating Curve Algorithm | Parametric reset curve mapping outdoor temp to supply temp setpoint |

#### L5.4.4: BUS Communication & Gateway

Data backbone connecting controller, thermostats, zone controllers, and external systems.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.4.4.1: BUS Interface Module | OpenTherm, eBUS, or Modbus transceiver on appliance PCB or add-on |
| L4.4.4.2: Internet Gateway / Cloud Connector | Wi-Fi or LAN gateway for remote monitoring, OTA updates, demand-response |

---

sections.title: L6.5 — Domestic Hot Water (DHW)
sections.content:
Dedicated subsystems for potable water heating. Combi boilers produce DHW instantaneously; system boilers use indirect cylinders.

### L5 Systems under L6.5

#### L5.5.1: DHW Storage Cylinder

Insulated vessel (150–300 L residential) heated by internal coil from boiler circuit.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.5.1.1: Vessel & Coil Assembly | Enamelled steel or stainless-steel vessel with one or two internal coils |
| L4.5.1.2: Insulation & Cladding | Rigid PU foam or EPS insulation; standing loss <1.5 kWh/24h for 200 L |
| L4.5.1.3: Anode Protection | Magnesium sacrificial anode or impressed-current anode for corrosion protection |

#### L5.5.2: Instantaneous DHW Heat Exchanger

Secondary plate heat exchanger in combi boilers heating potable water on demand.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.5.2.1: Plate Heat Exchanger (Water-to-Water) | Brazed stainless-steel plate pack; primary from boiler, secondary potable |
| L4.5.2.2: DHW Flow Sensor & Temperature Sensor | Detects tap opening; controls burner modulation to maintain outlet setpoint |
| L4.5.2.3: Thermostatic Mixing Valve | Blends hot + cold to prevent scalding; set to 48–55 °C |

#### L5.5.3: Legionella Protection

Periodic thermal disinfection raising cylinder to >60 °C.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.5.3.1: Immersion Heater Element | Electric element (1–3 kW) for legionella pasteurisation |
| L4.5.3.2: Disinfection Timer & Controller | Weekly programmable cycle with temperature confirmation |

---

sections.title: L6.6 — Installation, Commissioning & Aftermarket (H)
sections.content:
Horizontal process domain. ~30–40% of hydronic heating performance issues originate in installation/commissioning errors, not equipment. [SRC: web_search]

### L5 Systems under L6.6

#### L5.6.1: Installation Tooling

Specialised tools for system installation: pipe preparation, flue fitting, system flushing.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.6.1.1: Pipe Preparation & Joining Tools | Press-fit jaws, pipe cutters, deburring tools (copper, PEX, multilayer) |
| L4.6.1.2: Flue Installation Tools | Core drill, flue template jig, silicone sealant, fire-stop collar |
| L4.6.1.3: System Flushing Equipment | Power-flush machine for removing debris, flux residue, magnetite |

#### L5.6.2: Commissioning & Start-Up

Verification, parameterisation, and performance validation before occupant handover.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.6.2.1: Hydraulic Balancing Procedure | Setting flow rates per zone using manifold valves and flow meters |
| L4.6.2.2: Combustion Analysis | Flue-gas analyser verifying CO, CO2, O2, and stack temperature for compliance |
| L4.6.2.3: Compliance Documentation | Benchmark certificate, commissioning checklist, gas safety record |

#### L5.6.3: Aftermarket Service & Spare Parts

Ongoing maintenance, warranty, and spare parts.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.6.3.1: Annual Service Inspection | Gas safety check, combustion analysis, HX inspection, filter clean, inhibitor top-up |
| L4.6.3.2: Spare Parts Supply Chain | OEM and aftermarket parts via wholesalers (Wolseley, Rexel, Sonepar, Ferguson) |
| L4.6.3.3: Predictive Maintenance Platform | Cloud analytics consuming sensor data (incl. flow) for early fault detection |

---

sections.title: L6.7 — Energy Monitoring & Compliance (H)
sections.content:
Connects heating system to metering, efficiency verification, and grid integration. Driven by EcoDesign/ErP labelling mandates and energy audit requirements.

### L5 Systems under L6.7

#### L5.7.1: Thermal Energy Meter (Heat Meter)

MID-certified instrument measuring delivered thermal energy: Q = integral(flow x density x cp x delta-T) dt.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.7.1.1: Flow Sensor (Metering-Grade) | Ultrasonic or mechanical flow sensor with MID certification (EN 1434) |
| L4.7.1.2: Matched Temperature Sensor Pair | Pt500 or Pt1000 paired sensors for supply/return with <0.02 K matching error |
| L4.7.1.3: Integrator / Calculator Unit | Microprocessor integrating thermal power over time; stores cumulative kWh/MWh |

#### L5.7.2: Efficiency Verification

Monitoring and reporting systems for regulatory compliance.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.7.2.1: ErP / EcoDesign Label Module | Firmware computing seasonal efficiency metrics for regulatory labelling |
| L4.7.2.2: Data Logging & Export | Local or cloud storage of operating data for energy audits and warranty claims |

---

sections.content:

Baseboard and radiant floor are emitter-only products (no integrated heat generation); they consume hot water from a boiler. Pool heaters have simplified controls (setpoint only). Solar thermal contributes to DHW pre-heat but is rarely the sole heat source.

sections.title: Marquardt Position in the Value Network
sections.content:
```
L7   Hydronic Heating Equipment Ecosystem
 |
 +-- L6.2  Hydraulic Distribution
      |
      +-- L5.2.5  Flow Sensing & Measurement
           |
           +-- L4.2.5.1  Ultrasonic Flow Sensor  <-- MARQUARDT
           +-- L4.2.5.2  Impeller / Turbine Flow Sensor (incumbent)
           +-- L4.2.5.3  Vortex Flow Sensor (niche)
           +-- L4.2.5.4  Differential Pressure Estimation (budget fallback)
```

**Full L-path:** L7 > L6.2 Hydraulic Distribution > L5.2.5 Flow Sensing & Measurement > L4.2.5.1 Ultrasonic Flow Sensor

**Why this market:** Hydronic heating equipment (boilers, baseboard, radiant floor) operates in nearly identical conditions to the home-market heat pumps (NAICS 333415). Same pipe sizes (DN15–DN25), same media (water/glycol), same temperature range (2–90 °C), same OEM integration model. Architecture distance = 2. The technology transition from mechanical turbine sensors to ultrasonic is accelerated in this market by three forces: (1) EU EcoDesign requiring measured efficiency, not estimated; (2) OEM platform simplification demanding multi-variable sensing (flow + temperature + anomaly) in one package; (3) the shift to condensing boilers, where accurate flow measurement enables precise modulation and peak condensing efficiency. [SRC: 05_market_discovery, web_search]

Gas condensing boiler; Gas non-condensing boiler; Oil-fired boiler; Electric hydronic boiler; Hydronic baseboard heater; Radiant floor heating system; Solar water heating system; Pool/spa heater
Product VN (L7 through L4) — what subsystems compose hydronic heating appliances and their installation ecosystem
Weil-McLain, Burnham (U.S. Boiler Company), Navien, Rinnai, Bosch Thermotechnology, Bradford White, A.O. Smith, NTI Boilers, Slant/Fin, Laars (Pentair), Triangle Tube, Lochinvar, Viessmann

rows.Name: Heat Generation
rows.Description: Combustion, electric resistance, or solar thermal conversion subsystems that produce thermal energy in a hydronic medium. Technology choice here defines appliance type.

rows.Name: Hydraulic Distribution
rows.Description: Piping, pumping, sensing, and safety infrastructure moving heated water from source to emitters and back. **Marquardt sits here.**

rows.Name: Heat Emission
rows.Description: Terminal devices (baseboard, radiators, radiant floor, fan coils) transferring heat from water to occupied space.

rows.Name: Control & Regulation
rows.Description: Intelligence layer: appliance controllers, thermostats, zone valves, weather compensation, BUS communication.

rows.Name: Domestic Hot Water (DHW)
rows.Description: Potable water heating via storage cylinders, plate heat exchangers, thermostatic mixing, legionella protection.

rows.Name: Installation, Commissioning & Aftermarket
rows.Description: Human-process layer: installer tools, system flushing, commissioning, warranty service, spare parts.

rows.Name: Energy Monitoring & Compliance
rows.Description: Thermal energy metering, efficiency verification, ErP/EcoDesign labelling, smart-grid integration.
Modulating surface burner with metal-fibre or ceramic-fibre deck; air/gas premixing upstream
Modulating gas valve (Honeywell, SIT, Dungs), gas pressure regulator, shut-off valve
Hot-surface igniter or spark electrode; ionisation flame sensor for flame proving
Variable-speed EC blower providing forced draft and air/fuel ratio control
Stainless steel or Al-Si spiral/plate exchanger extracting latent heat from flue gases; achieves 95–98% efficiency
Cast iron, copper, or steel fire-tube/water-tube exchanger for sensible heat transfer; used in non-condensing boilers
Collects acidic condensate from flue-gas cooling; neutraliser required before drain discharge
Pressure-atomising nozzle (Danfoss, Delavan) converting fuel oil into fine spray
Gear pump with pressure regulator; pre-heater for viscous fuels
High-voltage transformer and spark electrodes for oil ignition
Incoloy or stainless steel sheathed element; typical 5–30 kW residential
Solid-state or mechanical contactors for multi-stage element switching
Manual-reset thermal cut-out preventing dry-fire or runaway heating
Selective-coated copper or aluminium absorber with copper riser tubes
Low-iron tempered glass cover; mineral wool or foam back insulation
Circulator, flow meter, non-return valve, pressure relief, and fill/drain valves in a compact unit
Room-sealed balanced-flue terminal allowing combustion air intake and exhaust via single wall penetration
Stainless steel or polypropylene (condensing-rated) duct sections
Calcium carbonate (limestone) cartridge neutralising acidic condensate to pH 5–7 before drain
Permanent-magnet electronically commutated motor with wet-rotor design; 4–80 W typical
Integrated inverter with 0–10 V, PWM, or BUS interface for external modulation
Cast iron or composite volute; DN20–DN32 threaded or flanged connections
Steel or stainless-steel chamber with internal baffles for flow decoupling and air/dirt separation
Automatic air vent (top); drain valve (bottom) for sludge removal
Thermal insulation shell preventing standing heat losses
Brass or stainless manifold with balancing valves and flow meters per circuit
Thermal or motor-driven actuators on each loop; respond to room thermostat signals
Wall-mount enclosure with condensation protection and thermal insulation
Copper or stainless steel from heat source to separator/manifold; typically DN22–DN28
PEX, PE-RT, or multilayer composite for zone circuits; DN12–DN20
Press fittings, compression fittings, solder joints; increasingly push-fit
Closed-cell elastomeric or polyethylene foam per EN 12828 / ASHRAE 90.1

**Transit-time ultrasonic measurement using opposed piezoelectric transducers. No moving parts, zero pressure drop, glycol-tolerant, air/empty-pipe detection. Marquardt DN20 is the reference product. Growing adoption in modern condensing boilers.**
Mechanical paddle-wheel or turbine rotor; incumbent in many boiler platforms. Moving parts subject to wear and scaling. Declining share.
Bluff-body vortex shedding; higher accuracy at mid-range flows but introduces pressure drop. Niche.
Inferred from pump speed and delta-P; zero hardware cost but low accuracy (±15–20%). Budget fallback.
Diaphragm vessel absorbing volumetric expansion; pre-charged nitrogen; 8–18 L residential
Spring-loaded valve at 3 bar (typical); discharges to tundish on overpressure
Analogue gauge for visual check; electronic transducer for controller alarms
Maintains system pressure; prevents potable water contamination per EN 1717
In-line magnetic filter capturing magnetite and ferrous debris
Mesh or cyclone filter for non-ferrous particles; installed on return line
Corrosion/scale inhibitor dosed at commissioning and annual service

Time-programme scheduling; self-learning occupancy in smart variants

Pt500 or Pt1000 paired sensors for supply/return with <0.02 K matching error
